title: Dog Snapping at Invisible Flies: Understanding Fly Biting Syndrome
meta_desc: Is your dog snapping at thin air? Discover what fly biting syndrome means, when to see a vet, and why recording the behavior is essential for triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Fly biting is a behavior where dogs snap at invisible insects, often signaling underlying neurological or gastrointestinal issues. While usually not an immediate emergency, you should schedule a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours and bring a video of the behavior to help with diagnosis.

What is fly biting syndrome in dogs and what causes it?

Fly biting, also known as star gazing or air snapping, is a behavior where a dog suddenly snaps their jaws at the air as if they are trying to catch a nonexistent insect. While it may occasionally look like play, it is frequently a clinical sign of an underlying medical condition. It is often categorized as a focal seizure or a symptom of gastrointestinal distress rather than a simple behavioral quirk.

When should I seek emergency veterinary care for my dog's fly biting?

  • The urgency level for fly biting is generally classified as Medium.
  • It is not typically a life-threatening emergency unless your dog is unable to stop, appears disoriented, or collapses.
  • Because this behavior often points to neurological issues or significant digestive discomfort, you should schedule a veterinary examination within the next 24 to 48 hours.

How does capturing a video of the behavior help my veterinarian?

  • Capturing a video of the snapping episode is one of the most helpful tools you can provide to a veterinary team.
  • Because these episodes are often intermittent, your dog may not exhibit the behavior during a physical exam.
  • A clear video allows the vet to observe the dog's facial expressions, eye movement, and level of awareness.
  • These details are critical for distinguishing between a behavioral compulsion, a focal seizure, and a physical reaction to pain.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Neurologic disorders, such as focal seizures or neuropathy, may manifest as fly snapping behavior in dogs. When physical examination, history, and diagnostic testing do not clearly identify the cause, a therapeutic response trial using anticonvulsants may be indicated to exclude focal seizures. Clomipramine or fluoxetine may also be used to rule out compulsive disorders. Other neurotransmitters like dopamine, endorphins, and N-methyl-D-aspartic acid (NMDA) may also play a role. Medical problems should be excluded as the underlying cause of these signs.

Chapter: Neurology, Infectious Disease, Dermatology, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 974)

faqs.question: Can fly biting be caused by allergies?
faqs.answer: While less common than neurological or GI causes, skin sensitivities or eye irritations can sometimes cause a dog to snap at their environment, but a vet must rule out seizures first.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of air snapping?
faqs.answer: The most frequent causes identified by veterinarians are focal seizures (epilepsy) and gastrointestinal disorders like acid reflux or esophageal discomfort.

faqs.question: Is fly biting common in specific breeds?
faqs.answer: While it can happen to any dog, it is frequently reported in Cavalier King Charles Spaniels and certain Terrier breeds, often linked to neurological predispositions.
